1:
The chapter opens the roll of the temple singers, and the witnesses divide their attention between the office itself and the arresting word chosen to describe it — that the sons of Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun were set apart "to prophesy with harps, with psalteries, and with cymbals." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays out the framework: David and the captains of the host, the same princes who ordered the priestly classes at 1 Chronicles 23:2 and 1 Chronicles 24:6, separated three guilds of sacred minstrels, famous to all after times, in whom every branch of Levi was represented — Asaph of Gershon, Heman of Kohath, Jeduthun of Merari, as 1 Chronicles 6:33 shows. The Hebrew verb "prophesy," he notes, is a reflexive form implying utterance under a spiritual influence; the ancients reckoned moving music an effect and proof of inspiration, a "gift" whose products come from the Divine source of all that makes life glad, as James 1:17 and 1 Samuel 10:5 suggest.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry meets the same word and insists on its restraint: singing God's praise is here called prophesying not because every singer foresaw things to come — though Heman is called the king's seer at v.5 — but because the psalms they sang were composed by prophets and were often prophetical, and because in Samuel's day the praising of God went by that very name, as at 1 Samuel 19:20, perhaps the sense of the prophesying Paul speaks of in 1 Corinthians 14:24. Henry fastens too on the twin words "service" and "workmen": it is the greatest liberty and pleasure to praise God, yet in our state of corruption it will not be done as it should without labour and struggle, so that we must take pains with our hearts to bring them to the work. The glory of God, he adds, was principally intended in all this temple-music, vocal or instrumental — a music of harps, psalteries, and cymbals suited to the temper of that dispensation, though those who would carry such concerts into worship now, he warns, will find that none pretend to it, remembering the idle players of Amos 6:5.

The wesleyan-arminian voices keep the plainer sense. Adam Clarke reads "should prophesy" as accompanying the instruments with prayer and singing, and calls David and the captains the chiefs of the several orders, not military officers; John Wesley, agreeing that the princes gathered at 1 Chronicles 23:2 are fitly styled the captains of the host, takes "prophecy" as praising God by singing the psalms of David and other sacred songs made by prophets and holy men. The reformed John Gill adds that the setting apart was done before the assembled princes and chief priests, the same court before whom the lots had been cast for the priestly and Levitical courses, and now for the singers. The witnesses thus set the frame Henry and Ellicott alike press home: the making of music before the LORD is real work and a real gift, ordered by the king yet owned by the Spirit, and directed wholly to the praise of God.
2:
The register begins with the house of Asaph — Zaccur, Joseph, Nethaniah, and Asarelah — and the witnesses use the first-named guild to press the question the word "prophesy" keeps raising. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke gives the plain gloss, that "which prophesied" means they sung hymns and prayed; but he will not let the plainer reading stand alone, for the Targum understands it in the proper sense, "Who prophesied by the Holy Spirit," and Jarchi agrees, citing the case of Elisha at 2 Kings 3:15, where, while the minstrel played, the hand of the LORD — that is, the spirit of prophecy — came upon him. So the older Jewish expositors read a literal inspiration where the moderns read devout song.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott stands with the musical sense: the participle "which prophesied," the same form as in v.1, means simply that they made music, and the four leaders here named were trained members of the guild, subordinate to Asaph and under his direction. He pauses on a name: Asarelah is spelt Jesharelah at v.14, and Ewald would identify it with "Israel," the unaccented ending having the force of "unto," so that the name is in effect the modern surname Israels. Keil and Delitzsch, of the same grammatical-historical school, attend to the little phrase "at the hand of Asaph": it expresses no different relation than "at the hands of the king" does, signifying only "under the direction of," for the musical worship was arranged by David and the three chief musicians in common, though only the latter performed it.

The reformed John Gill marks that the sons of Asaph were separated and by lot appointed to the singers' service, and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads "of Asaph" as under his direction and "of the king" as in such manner and order as David appointed. The voices divide, then, not over who these men were but over what they did when they "prophesied": whether, with the Targum and Jarchi, the Spirit of prophecy actually rested on the players as it had on Elisha, or whether, with Clarke's own gloss, Ellicott, and Wesley, the word names the inspired art of sacred song — praise offered under a spiritual influence, but not the foretelling of things to come.
3:
Jeduthun's house comes next — Gedaliah, Zeri, Jeshaiah, Hashabiah, and Mattithiah — and the witnesses stumble at once on the number, for the text says "six" while it lists only five. The wesleyan-arminian voices reach for the simplest cure. Adam Clarke reads the six as five sons together with their father, so that Jeduthun himself completes the tally, which is why the verse adds that they were "under the hands of their father Jeduthun, who prophesied with a harp"; John Wesley offers the same, that Jeduthun is included in the number — though he allows the alternative, that the missing sixth is Shimei, named later at v.17.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses that second answer as the true one. Only five names are now read, he says, because the name of Shimei has fallen out — the one name in the whole roll of the lots at vv.9-31 that does not appear here in vv.2-4 — and the Alexandrian Greek actually inserts it between Jeshaiah and Hashabiah. He corrects a spelling too: the Zeri here is probably the Izri of v.11, an error as old as the ancient versions, while Jeshaiah is only another form of Isaiah. Keil and Delitzsch note the same discrepancy without resolving it, observing that the number is given for Jeduthun and Heman but withheld for Asaph, and that Jeduthun's name is placed first in the verse because the choir he led bore his name.

The reformed John Gill adds only that Jeduthun is the man elsewhere called Ethan, and that these are his sons. So the witnesses leave a genuine fork: either the "six" counts the father at the head of his five sons, as Clarke and Wesley prefer, or a sixth son's name — Shimei — has genuinely dropped from the text and must be restored from the parallel list, as Ellicott, Keil, and Wesley's second thought all suppose. The verse also fixes the purpose of the whole guild in a clause the witnesses do not dispute: Jeduthun's sons prophesied with the harp "to give thanks and to praise the LORD," the plain end, as at 1 Chronicles 16:4, of all their sacred music.
4:
Heman's house is the largest — fourteen sons — and here the witnesses meet the chapter's most curious feature, for the closing names, read in order, seem to compose a sentence. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott points it out with wonder: the last six names, excluding the odd "Joshbekashah," form as they stand a complete poetical couplet, which may be rendered, "God has come; I have exalted and extolled the help; I have spoken abundance of visions." Such words, he observes, are very suitable in the mouth of a seer, as Heman is called in the next verse — and yet he checks himself, allowing that the arrangement of the names in this order is perhaps only a mnemonic device. Keil and Delitzsch report the fuller form of the same idea from Ewald, who held that the utterance of a great prophet lies here cut up into the names of near relatives: the words behind Giddalti, Romamti-ezer, Mahazioth, Hothir, and Mallothi yield the verse "I have given great and lofty help, I have to fulness spoken oracles," which may have been the opening of a famed ancient oracle, transferred to the five musical sons of Heman.

So the debate is real and delicate: are these names a hidden prayer or prophecy, deliberately woven into the family, or a household of ordinary names that only happen, when strung together, to fall into verse? Ellicott holds both possibilities open, leaning to caution; Keil and Delitzsch pass on Ewald's bolder claim that a genuine oracle underlies them. Either way the witnesses agree the couplet is no accident of the ear alone — the two verbs giddalti and romamti, "I have nourished and brought up," stand together in Isaiah 1:2, and the names ring with help exalted and visions spoken.

Ellicott adds the smaller identifications the lot-list will need. Uzziel, "power of God" as at Exodus 6:18, is the Azareel of v.18, the words differing in Hebrew by a single letter; Shebuel here is the Shubael of v.20; and Giddalti and Romamti-ezer may perhaps be read as Giddalti-ezer and Romamti-ezer, though Giddalti stands alone again at v.29. The reformed John Gill, for his part, is content to name Heman simply as the third chief singer whose sons these are. The witnesses thus turn a bare list into the chapter's strangest marvel: a family register that reads, to those who have ears for the Hebrew, like the broken half of a psalm.
5:
The subscription to Heman's list lifts him above a mere choirmaster: "all these were the sons of Heman the king's seer in the words of God, to lift up the horn," and God gave him fourteen sons and three daughters. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dwells on the title. "The king's seer in the words of God" means in divine, that is liturgical, matters; Heman was a prophet as well as a minstrel, for music and prophecy stand close together, as at 1 Samuel 10:5 and 2 Kings 3:15. The word is hozeh, a "gazer," different from the ro'eh rendered seer at 1 Samuel 9:9; as Gad was David's seer at 1 Chronicles 21:9, so Jeduthun is the king's seer at 2 Chronicles 35:15.

Over the next phrase the witnesses genuinely part. Adam Clarke, of the wesleyan-arminian school, takes "to lift up the horn" quite literally, following Jarchi's "horn of prophecy" — to sound the trumpet in the words of prophecy before the LORD; John Wesley likewise hears an instrument of horn, a martial music grateful to David's martial spirit. But Ellicott objects that the horn appears nowhere else among the Temple instruments, and that "to lift up the horn" of a person is a well-known Hebrew metaphor, as in Hannah's song at 1 Samuel 2:10, "he shall exalt the horn of his anointed." On his reading the sense is that God gave all these "sons" — proficient disciples — to Heman in order to strengthen him for his work with a strong body of able assistants.

The reformed John Gill keeps to the title, naming Heman the prophet of the king who was frequently with him; and on the three daughters the witnesses agree with quiet interest. Clarke notes that they too were employed among the singers, and Ellicott finds in them an indication that women sustained a part in the service of song, as Miriam and the women after her did at Exodus 15:20 and 1 Samuel 18:6. Keil and Delitzsch add the grammarian's caution that "all these" in the following verse reaches back over the whole company of Asaph, Jeduthun, and Heman, not Heman's fourteen alone. So the verse leaves Heman doubly honoured — a seer whose gift was song, blessed with a houseful of children who carried that gift on — while the witnesses dispute only whether the horn he lifted was a trumpet in his hand or a strength given him from God.
6:
The verse gathers the three houses into one body of worship: all these were under the hands of their father for song in the house of the LORD, with cymbals, psalteries, and harps, according to the king's order to Asaph, Jeduthun, and Heman. The reformed John Gill reads it as a note of instruction and discipline — the singers were taught and directed by their father in the performance of the temple service, each son formed in his art under the eye of the man who led his guild.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the phrasing and the sense. "All these," he insists, are the twenty-four leading minstrels enumerated in vv.2-4, and each performed under the supervision of his own "father," that is, his director; the instruments are better given as cymbals, harps, and lutes. The closing clause he would render not "according to the king's order to Asaph, Jeduthun, and Heman" but "under the directions of the king, and Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun" together — for the whole arrangement of the minstrels' duties was accomplished by David with the help of his three chief musicians, exactly as, in the ordering of the priestly courses, the king had been assisted by the chief priests Zadok and Ahimelech at 1 Chronicles 24:3.

The witnesses thus make the verse the seal of the whole preceding list, and its lesson lies in the pairing of authority they both feel. The service is ordered from the throne, yet it runs through the fathers of the guilds; the king plans it, but he plans it with the masters of the music, and the sons learn it at their own fathers' hands. It is a picture of worship neither improvised nor imposed — a settled institution, handed down within families and governed by a king who takes counsel with those who know the craft. Gill's stress on the sons trained under their father and Ellicott's on the king ordering all with the three chiefs meet in a single portrait: the praise of the house of God is a work of ordered succession, in which each generation is taught its part, and each singer stands, at once, under his father, under the chief musician, and under the king who set the whole in motion for the glory of God.
7:
The roll is now totalled: "the number of them, with their brethren that were instructed in the songs of the LORD, even all that were cunning, was two hundred fourscore and eight." The witnesses do the arithmetic and find in it the architecture of the whole choir. Adam Clarke, of the wesleyan-arminian school, resolves the figure at once — 288 is twelve classes of twenty-four Levites each, for the number divides evenly by twelve — while John Wesley explains that these "cunning" men were so skilful they could teach others, and together with their scholars made up the four thousand appointed to the service of song at 1 Chronicles 23:5.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the same total the other way about, as twenty-four multiplied by twelve: each of the twenty-four leaders named as the "sons" of Asaph, Jeduthun, and Heman in vv.2-4 was associated with a company of eleven brethren, experts in the chanting of the sanctuary, and it was the twenty-four leaders who accompanied their choirs with instrumental music. The reformed John Gill fills in the same picture, that each of the chief singers had twelve with him, himself and eleven more, as the following verses will reckon them out one by one.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, look forward to the casting of the lots that determines the order of service, and pause over the difficult phrase that governs it — the "ward against ward," which some would read as "class like class" by repeating the word, but which the accents bind rather to the lots themselves, since the classes did not yet exist to be compared until the lots had made them. The witnesses agree that the number is not idle: 288 trained masters, drawn from a far larger body of four thousand, stand as the skilled core of Israel's praise, each set to lead a company of eleven, so that the whole service of song rests on a small band of the excellent who were able not only to sing but to teach. The economy is deliberate — a competent number, all of them cunning in the work, to keep up the service in the house of God.
8:
"And they cast lots, ward against ward, as well the small as the great, the teacher as the scholar" — and the witnesses find in the lot the chapter's quiet doctrine of humility. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes it his theme. The four-and-twenty leaders, sons of Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun, were all qualified for the service and called to it; the only question was the order in which they should serve, and this was settled by lot to prevent the strife for precedency, a sin, he warns, that most easily besets even good people. The lot was thrown impartially — the men placed in twenty-four companies of twelve, in two rows, and the lots cast ward against ward, putting them all upon a level, small and great, teacher and scholar alike. They did not go by age, nor by standing, nor by degrees taken in the music-schools, but referred it to God; for small and great, teachers and scholars, stand alike before him who does not go by our rules of distinction and precedency, as the Lord teaches at Matthew 20:23.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the same equality in its Hebrew and its history. They cast "lots of charge," he explains, to determine the order in which each of the twenty-four guilds should take charge of the services — the Septuagint's "lots of courses," the very word behind the priestly course of Luke 1:6. "As well the small as the great" means exactly that the younger houses had no advantage over the elder, the order being decided wholly by lot; and "the teacher as the scholar" sets the cunning master beside the learner. He notes a philological curiosity: the word for scholar, talmid, occurs nowhere else in the Old Testament — the very term the Talmud uses for a rabbi's disciple, the exact equivalent of the New Testament's "disciple." From 1 Chronicles 23:5 he reckons that these 288 masters trained a rank and file of some three thousand seven hundred more.

The reformed John Gill reads the "ward against ward" concretely, as fixing which course of the singers should attend upon which course of the priests in the weekly temple service, and John Wesley, of the wesleyan-arminian school, agrees that the ward was a course of Levites answerable to one of the priests, as at 1 Chronicles 23:28, the lot drawn without respect to their different ages or abilities. The witnesses converge on Henry's lesson: before God the great singer and the raw learner cast the same lot, and the God who orders his house by no seniority of ours teaches even his musicians to take their place by his appointment and not their own.
9:
The drawing begins: "the first lot came forth for Asaph to Joseph; the second to Gedaliah." The witnesses read the opening pair for what it reveals about the whole procedure. The reformed John Gill notes that the first lot fell to Joseph, the second son of Asaph, so that the younger was preferred before the firstborn from the very start — a hint, dropped at once, that the lot would not follow the order of birth. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, confirms from v.2 that Joseph was Asaph's second son, and adds the clause that must be silently supplied to every name in the list, though it is written out only here and there — that "he, and his sons, and his brethren, were twelve" — for the total of 288 requires each of the twenty-four to head a company of twelve. The "brethren," he explains, are the fellow-clansmen and the "sons" the subordinate disciples, unless we are to think of elder and younger families grouped together in one class.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, trace the striking pattern the drawing produced. The four sons of Asaph drew the first, third, fifth, and seventh places; the sons of Jeduthun the second, fourth, and other even lots; and the sons of Heman the sixth, ninth, eleventh, and thirteenth, while all the remaining places from the fifteenth on fell to Heman's house alone. From this they conclude that the lots were not sorted into separate urns and drawn by turns, but that all were cast together into one, and that it simply so fell out that after the fourteenth drawing only sons of Heman were left. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley makes the same point about the supplied clause, that "he, his sons, and his brethren were twelve" must be understood throughout, else the numbers will not reach the two hundred and eighty-eight of v.7.

A small discord runs under the agreement: Adam Clarke calls Joseph the firstborn of Asaph and Gedaliah the firstborn of Jeduthun, where Gill and Ellicott make Joseph the second son. But the witnesses are one on the substance the verse teaches — that the order of Israel's singers was fixed by a lot cast into a single urn and left wholly to God, so that a second son might lead and a first son follow, and no house could claim its rank by nature rather than by the divine disposing.
10:
"The third to Zaccur, he, his sons, and his brethren, twelve" — and with the third lot the witnesses step back to survey the run of names the drawing now unrolls. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, treat this verse as the head of a block that carries the count forward through the middle lots, from Zaccur the third down the list, each leader with his company of twelve, until the sons of Heman are left to fill the remaining places. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, fixes the form of words that will now repeat to the end: literally "the third, Zaccur and his sons and his brethren — twelve," a construction held steady through the following verses.

Ellicott lingers over one exception that betrays the nature of these entries. At the next lot the text reads not "the fourth to Izri" but "the fourth for the Izrite," a Gentilic form; and this, he urges, shows that we are dealing here with families or clans rather than bare individuals — the "son" of a guild standing for a whole house that bore the founder's name. The reminder governs the whole roll: Zaccur and the rest are at once persons and the heads of enduring companies, their names carried on by the eleven brethren counted with each.

The two grammatical-historical witnesses leave the arithmetic to speak, for the pattern is now plain: the third of the twenty-four courses falls to Zaccur, a son of Asaph, and the steady refrain "he, his sons, and his brethren, twelve" tolls on through the chapter like the counting of a muster. What they draw from so mechanical a verse is the seriousness with which Israel numbered its singers — not a crowd loosely gathered for a festival, but a fixed and ordered establishment, twenty-four courses of twelve, each in its appointed turn, so that the praise of the house of God should never fail nor fall into confusion, but roll on, course after course, as regularly as the priests at the altar. Zaccur's single line is a link in that unbroken chain, and the Chronicler sets it down with the same care he gave the royal genealogy, that the servants of the sanctuary's song might be remembered name by name.
11:
"The fourth to Izri, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve." The witnesses have only a word to spare for this lot, and they spend it on the name. Adam Clarke, of the wesleyan-arminian school, identifies the man: Izri is the second son of Jeduthun, the same whose name stood as Zeri back in v.3, so that the fourth of the twenty-four courses is drawn from the house of Jeduthun.

The variant spelling is itself a small witness to how these names travelled. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, had already observed that Zeri and Izri are one, an interchange as old as the ancient versions, and that the form here is really "the Izrite," a Gentilic name denoting a family rather than a single person. The fourth course, then, is a clan of Jeduthun's line, called after its founder as a whole house might be.

Slight as the verse is, it falls into the larger design the earlier witnesses traced. Jeduthun's sons, Keil and Delitzsch had noted, drew the even places in the drawing — the second, the fourth, and so on — while Asaph's took the odd; and here the pattern holds, the fourth lot coming to a Jeduthunite. There is nothing to expound and nothing to dispute; the verse simply records that when the fourth lot was drawn from the urn it bore the name of Izri, and that he, with his eleven brethren, made up the twelve who were to take their turn in the song of the house of the LORD. The witnesses let it stand as one more bead on the string, content that a name which might otherwise have perished is kept in the register of those who served God with the harp — Izri the son of Jeduthun, the fourth course of the temple singers, remembered for nothing but that he was called and counted. Yet even that is not nothing; for the house of Jeduthun had been set apart, as v.3 declared, to prophesy with the harp for the giving of thanks and praise, and Izri's course was one of the four-and-twenty channels through which that praise was to flow.
12:
"The fifth to Nethaniah, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve." Here the witnesses catch the moment the drawing shows its hand. Adam Clarke, of the wesleyan-arminian school, names Nethaniah the third son of Asaph, and then draws the general observation the sequence has been urging: "thus we find the lot did not run in any particular kind of order." Asaph's second son had drawn the first lot, his eldest the third, and now his third son the fifth, so that no rule of age or rank can be read off the result.

That disorder is, to the witnesses, the very point. The lot was cast precisely to take the choice out of human hands and lay it in God's; and God, ordering the places by no seniority of ours, scattered the sons of each house up and down the list as it pleased him. The fifth course falls to a son of Asaph, the third by birth, set between his brothers who drew earlier and later; and the seeming randomness is the fingerprint of a disposing that owns no obligation to our expectations.

Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, keeps the count in its steady form — "the fifth, Nethaniah, his sons and his brethren, twelve" — the same construction that holds from the third lot to the last. That very steadiness of the wording, repeated name after name, throws the irregularity of the outcome into sharper relief: the form is fixed, but the order it records is not, and the fixity of the one only underlines that the disposing of the other lay elsewhere than in men's hands. The witnesses add nothing to the name itself, for nothing more is known of this Nethaniah than that he was a singer of Asaph's house who drew the fifth of the twenty-four courses. But in Clarke's small remark they find the lesson the whole list has been quietly teaching: that the order of God's servants is God's to appoint, and that the lot which lifted a younger son over an elder, and set the fifth course in the hands of Asaph's third-born, was no accident but the hidden choice of the LORD, into whose hand the lot and its whole disposing are given. So even a bare line of the register preaches, without a word of exhortation, the sovereignty that governs the smallest turn of the temple's order.
14:
"The seventh to Jesharelah, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve." The seventh lot closes the run of Asaph's four sons, and the witnesses pause chiefly over the shifting shape of the name. Adam Clarke, of the wesleyan-arminian school, supposes Jesharelah to be the same as Uzziel, a son of Heman — though the identification sits awkwardly, for Jesharelah belongs, by the reckoning of the other witness, to the house of Asaph and not of Heman.

Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, had traced the name more surely at v.2, where it appears in the fuller form Asarelah among Asaph's sons: Jesharelah is only the same name worn smooth, which Ewald would read as "Israel," the ending carrying the force of "unto," so that it amounts to the surname Israels. The two forms, Asarelah and Jesharelah, are one man, the youngest of Asaph's four, here drawing the seventh course; the difference of a syllable, he grants, is no real difference of person, for the same Levite is meant however the scribes spelled him.

So the witnesses leave a small tangle over identity — Clarke pulling the name toward Heman, Ellicott anchoring it in Asaph — but they agree on the fact the verse records, that with the seventh lot the sons of Asaph are all provided for, having drawn the first, third, fifth, and seventh places, the odd numbers of the drawing, while the even fell to Jeduthun and the later lots to Heman. With this seventh lot, then, the first of the three guilds is fully told out; Asaph's four sons have taken their courses, and the drawing turns to complete Jeduthun's house and to work through the long roll of Heman's. The name's very instability is a reminder of how these registers were copied and recopied, a single singer appearing now as Asarelah and now as Jesharelah, yet held fast in the record for all that. And so the seventh course of the temple song is set down in his name, that he too might be numbered among those whom David and the captains of the host set apart to prophesy with harps and psalteries and cymbals in the house of the LORD, his one distinction being that he was chosen and enrolled among the singers of God.
17:
"The tenth to Shimei, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve." This unremarkable lot carries a quiet vindication, and the witnesses seize it. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, points out that Shimei is the name "omitted by accident from v.3" — the sixth son of Jeduthun who had dropped out of the roll of his father's house, leaving only five names where the text had said six. Here, in the drawing of the lots, he reappears; the missing man is not missing after all, but takes his place as the tenth of the twenty-four courses.

The recovery settles the puzzle the earlier verse had raised. When the list of Jeduthun's sons named only five and yet reckoned them six, some had supposed the father himself was counted in, while others judged that a name had simply fallen from the text — and it is precisely this Shimei, present in the lot-list at the tenth place but absent from v.3, who shows the latter to be right. The single name that appears among the lots of vv.9-31 without a match in the roll of vv.2-4 is his, and the Alexandrian Greek even restores him to his father's house between Jeshaiah and Hashabiah.

So a bare line of the register becomes, in Ellicott's hands, a small triumph of the text over its own scribes. Shimei of Jeduthun, accidentally lost from the family list, is held fast in the list of the courses; the tenth lot keeps what the third let slip. The witness finds in it an unlooked-for confirmation that the chapter's numbers can be trusted even where its names have stumbled — the "six" of v.3 was right, and the sixth son stood here all along, drawn in his turn to lead a course of twelve in the giving of thanks and praise to the LORD. What the eye might pass over as mere repetition proves, on a closer reading, to guard the integrity of the whole roll, so that not one of the four-and-twenty singers is finally lost, however a copyist's slip may once have hidden him.
18:
"The eleventh to Azareel, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve." The eleventh course belongs to a son of Heman, and Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, spends his note on reconciling the name with the earlier roll: this Azareel is the Uzziel named among Heman's fourteen sons at v.4. The two forms differ in the Hebrew by a single letter, and the shift is of a common kind — the same interchange, he observes, by which Azariah stands as a variant of Uzziah at 1 Chronicles 3:12 and 2 Chronicles 26:1.

The identification matters because it keeps the count honest. Heman's sons must all be found again among the lots, and a name that has changed its dress between the family list and the roll of courses might otherwise seem a stranger. Ellicott's care to match Azareel with Uzziel — as he had matched Shubael with Shebuel, and would match Jeremoth with Jerimoth — is of a piece with his whole reading of the chapter, in which the same men recur under slightly altered spellings, and the reader must hold the parallel columns together to see that none is lost and none doubled.

Uzziel's name means "the power of God," as at Exodus 6:18, and Azareel, "God has helped" — two namings of a single grace, that the strength and the help by which a man serves are alike from God. The eleventh course of the singers is set in his name, one of the fourteen sons whom God gave to Heman to lift up the horn; and whether he is called Uzziel in the family or Azareel in the lot, he is the same singer, drawn in his turn to lead his eleven brethren in the music of the house of God. The witness leaves the verse there, a single line whose only difficulty is a letter, and whose only lesson is the patience with which the register guards each name — a patience that answers, in its small way, to the faithfulness of the God who forgets none of his servants, though their very names come down to us worn and altered by the hands that copied them.
19:
"The twelfth to Hashabiah, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve." Of this lot the witnesses have almost nothing to say, and Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, offers only the barest textual note — that the reading "to Hashabiah" is simply what the Hebrew gives, the name standing without variant or difficulty. There is no puzzle here of a dropped letter or a shifted spelling, as with Shimei or Azareel; the name is plain, and Ellicott lets it pass with a word.

Yet the verse has its place in the design the witnesses have been tracing. Hashabiah is a son of Jeduthun, and the twelfth course thus falls, like the second and the fourth and the eighth before it, to Jeduthun's house — for the drawing, as Keil and Delitzsch had observed, gave the even-numbered places to the sons of Jeduthun and the odd to those of Asaph, until the later lots were left to Heman alone. The twelfth lot keeps that rhythm, a Jeduthunite drawing an even place near the midpoint of the four-and-twenty.

The very silence of the witnesses over a verse like this is instructive. Where there is a textual crux they crowd in with versions and conjectures; where the name stands clear they say only "so the Hebrew" and move on. Hashabiah asks for no defence, and receives none; he is one of the six sons of Jeduthun set apart, as v.3 had said, to prophesy with the harp for the giving of thanks and praise to the LORD, and his course is the twelfth of those that were to serve in their turns. The register keeps his name for that alone — that he was called of God to the song of the house, and that when the lot came out of the urn it appointed him and his eleven brethren to the twelfth of the temple's four-and-twenty watches of praise. It is enough, the witnesses seem to feel, that a man should be numbered among the singers of God; the record needs to say no more of him than his name and his turn.
20:
"The thirteenth to Shubael, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve." Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, uses the thirteenth lot to explain the Hebrew idiom that governs the rest of the list. The construction, he says, is really "as to the thirteenth, Shubael, his sons and his brethren, twelve" — the ordinal standing absolutely, "as regards" or "as concerns" the thirteenth lot, and the name following as the one on whom it fell. From here to the end of the chapter the phrasing runs in this clipped, almost administrative form, a ledger's shorthand for the drawing of the courses.

The name itself Ellicott recognises as one already met. Shubael is the Shebuel numbered among Heman's sons at v.4, and the same man appears in the wider Levitical lists at 1 Chronicles 23:16 and 1 Chronicles 24:20, where the house of Amram and its descendants are set down. The thirteenth course, then, is a Hemanite, drawn as the drawing began to leave the later places to Heman's large house.

There is no dispute for the witness to record, only the quiet work of matching a name to its other occurrences and construing the Hebrew that carries it. What the verse yields, under Ellicott's hand, is a glimpse of the register as a working document — its phrasing pared to the ordinal and the name, its Shubael identical with the Shebuel of the family roll, its thirteenth lot slotted into the same steady frame as the twelfth and the fourteenth. The same Shubael, Ellicott implies, is thus met more than once over in the Chronicler's lists, and the reader who follows the name from roll to roll sees how carefully the writer meant his registers to be cross-checked and held together. The singer so appointed takes his place among the sons of Heman whom God had given to lift up the horn, and his eleven brethren with him, to keep the thirteenth of the four-and-twenty courses in the song of the house of the LORD. The Chronicler asks no more of the verse than that it hold the name in its rank; Ellicott asks no more than that the name be rightly read, and rightly matched to the man who bore it in the other rolls of Levi.
22:
"The fifteenth to Jeremoth, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve." With the fifteenth lot the drawing passes wholly into the house of Heman, and Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, turns again to the wavering of a name across the manuscripts. The Hebrew here is "as to the fifteenth, to Jeremoth," the same construction that holds to the end of the chapter; and the name itself, he notes, hovers between Jeremoth and Jerimoth, the very form used of this son of Heman back at v.4. Spelling, and probably pronunciation, fluctuated between the two, as they do for the like names at 1 Chronicles 23:23 and 1 Chronicles 24:30.

Ellicott gathers the versions to show how far the wavering reached. The Septuagint and the Vulgate spell the name "Jerimoth" in both places here; the Syriac gives "Jarmuth"; the Arabic offers now one form and now another. The ancient translators, that is, met the same small instability the Hebrew shows, and each resolved it in his own way — a reminder that these names came down through many hands, and that the singers of Israel were remembered under forms that shifted slightly from tongue to tongue. Ellicott's patient collation of Greek, Latin, Syriac, and Arabic is no pedantry but a guard set over the text: by laying the versions side by side he makes sure that the single Hemanite behind all these spellings is neither lost from the roll nor mistaken for two men.

What the witnesses will not let the fluctuation obscure is the man beneath it. Whether he is written Jeremoth or Jerimoth, he is one of the fourteen sons of Heman, and the fifteenth of the twenty-four courses is his. By this point in the drawing the sons of Asaph and Jeduthun are all provided for, and every remaining lot — the fifteenth among them — falls to Heman's house, exactly as Keil and Delitzsch had foreseen when they judged that all the lots were cast into a single urn and that only Hemanites were left after the fourteenth was drawn. Jeremoth's course stands as one of those later lots, a name worn now this way and now that in the copying, yet held securely in the register of those set apart to prophesy with harps and psalteries and cymbals in the house of God.
24:
"The seventeenth to Joshbekashah, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve." The seventeenth course falls to one of the later-born sons of Heman, and the witnesses' interest in the name reaches back to the strange couplet those names compose. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, had marked Joshbekashah as the odd man in that arrangement: when the last of Heman's sons are read together as a kind of prayer or oracle — "I have exalted and extolled the help; I have spoken abundance of visions" — it is precisely this "peculiar Joshbekashah" that will not fit, the one name he must set aside to let the verse run smooth.

So the very name that breaks the pattern is worth its line. Where Giddalti, Romamti-ezer, Mahazioth, and their fellows can be construed as Hebrew words of praise and vision, Joshbekashah stands apart, resisting the pious reading — a reminder, if Ellicott's own caution is heeded, that the couplet may after all be a mnemonic accident rather than a designed oracle, since a genuine prophetic sentence would hardly have suffered so awkward a member in its midst. The name that spoils the prayer is quietly evidence against the prayer. Yet the argument cuts both ways, for a mere mnemonic device would have as little reason to admit so intractable a name; and Ellicott, honest to the difficulty, leaves the question hanging rather than force Joshbekashah into a sense it does not bear.

Matthew Henry, of the puritan-evangelical school, in his survey of the drawing, had set Joshbekashah in his place among the sons of Heman and his lot among the four-and-twenty, one of the many whom the impartial lot lifted or lowered without regard to birth. And Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, account for his falling here so late: by their reading all the lots were cast into one urn, and the sons of Asaph and Jeduthun having been drawn out first, the seventeenth and the remaining places were left to Heman's numerous house, of which Joshbekashah was one. The witnesses thus make even this bare entry serve the chapter's larger questions — whether Heman's sons were named to spell a prayer, and how the lot so distributed the houses — and they keep the name, unpronounceable and unprophetic as it is, among those appointed to the song of the LORD.
30:
"The three and twentieth to Mahazioth, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve." If Joshbekashah was the name that would not fit the prayer, Mahazioth is one of the names that make it, and the witnesses read the twenty-third lot in that light. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, had placed Mahazioth among the closing sons of Heman whose names, taken in order, compose a poetical couplet fit for the mouth of a seer; the word means "visions," and it furnishes the very phrase "I have spoken abundance of visions" with which the supposed oracle ends.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, report the fuller conjecture from Ewald, who held that a great prophet's utterance had been cut up into these family names — "I have given great and lofty help, I have to fulness spoken oracles" — with Mahazioth supplying the closing word of vision. Whether that is design or coincidence the witnesses will not finally decide; but they agree that this name, unlike its awkward neighbour, carries a clear and fitting sense, so that a son of the king's seer should be called "visions" is no surprise at all. The meaning of the name, indeed, is what lends the whole conjecture its force: it is because words like Mahazioth and Giddalti already bear the sense of vision and of greatness that they can be strung, with a little help, into the semblance of an oracle at all.

Matthew Henry, of the puritan-evangelical school, in his tally of the drawing, had reckoned Mahazioth among the fourteen sons of Heman and given his lot its place in the sequence, one more of the courses that the lot assigned by no rule of age. And why so late a lot should fall to him Keil and Delitzsch supply again from the mechanics of the urn: the sons of Asaph and Jeduthun drawn out first, the closing places were left to Heman's large house, and the twenty-third among them came to Mahazioth. The witnesses keep the name for its meaning as much as its rank — a singer called "visions," set to lead the twenty-third course in the house of God, and standing, whether by art or accident, as one syllable of that half-buried prayer which the sons of Heman seem to spell when their names are read together in the order the Chronicler set them down.
31:
The last lot closes the roll: "the four and twentieth to Romamti-ezer, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve." Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, stands back at the end to analyse the whole distribution. The first, third, fifth, and seventh places, he shows, fell to the four guilds of Asaph; the second, fourth, eighth, tenth, twelfth, and fourteenth to the six guilds of Jeduthun, the sons of Ethan; and the sixth, ninth, eleventh, thirteenth, together with all the remaining ten, to the fourteen guilds of Heman. It is evident, he concludes, that all the lots were thrown into a single urn, and that the names of Asaph and Jeduthun were all drawn — as the chances made likely — before those of Heman were exhausted, so that after the fourteenth drawing only Hemanite names were left. Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, had reached the identical conclusion from the same pattern, against Bertheau's supposition of separate urns drawn by turns.

Adam Clarke, of the wesleyan-arminian school, first settles the name — Romamti-ezer is a single name, both its parts belonging to the same person, the son of Heman already met at v.4 — and then breaks, startlingly, into open disapproval of the whole institution the chapter has described. "With this immense parade of noise and show," he writes, "(David's own invention), Christianity has nothing to do." It is the sharpest dissent in the chapter: where the other witnesses have expounded the temple music as a divinely ordered service, Clarke sets it down as a piece of Davidic display that the gospel church leaves behind.

His remark answers, from the far end of the chapter, a caution Matthew Henry had raised at the beginning — that the bringing of such concerts of instrumental music into worship now is what none pretend to. The two voices, puritan and Wesleyan, agree that the elaborate apparatus of harps and cymbals belonged to that dispensation and not to this; but Clarke goes further than Henry, denying it any warrant at all and laying it to David's own devising. The grammatical-historical witnesses, content to describe and to reckon, pass no such verdict; they leave the four-and-twenty courses as the Chronicler left them, a completed order of praise, and let Romamti-ezer's last lot ring down the register of the singers of the house of the LORD. So the chapter that opened with David and the captains setting apart the singers ends with the whole company numbered, ordered, and drawn by lot — and with the witnesses divided, at the last, over whether all this belonged only to the shadow, or whether the praise of God with the whole heart, and even the whole art, of a people has a place in every age.
